intro:
Gabbatha (meaning: high; elevated) was the Aramaic name for the stone pavement outside Herod's praetorium in Jerusalem where Pilate sat in judgment and delivered Jesus to be crucified (John 19:13). John notes that the place was called in Hebrew Gabbatha, while the Greek term was Lithostrotos (the pavement). Pilate brought Jesus out and sat on the judgment seat at this location, publicly pronouncing the sentence in the presence of the crowd that had gathered.

The identification of the site has been debated. Archaeological excavations beneath the Convent of the Sisters of Zion revealed a large stone pavement often associated with the Lithostrotos, but scholars dispute whether this predates or postdates the time of Jesus. Other scholars locate the praetorium at Herod's palace on the western hill, making its pavement the true Gabbatha. Whatever its precise location, the site represents the geographical and narrative fulcrum of John's passion account, where Roman judicial authority formally condemned the Son of God (John 18:28).
